title: Watching the Trains in Mumbai
summary: A first-time visitor describes spending a morning at a busy Mumbai station — the crowd, the chai sellers, and the quiet old man who notices everything.
body: When I visited Mumbai for the first time, I spent a whole morning at one of the city's big railway stations. I didn't take any train. I just wanted to watch. The platforms were full of people — students with heavy bags, workers in clean shirts, mothers holding children's hands very tightly. Trains arrived every few minutes, and they were always crowded. Some people jumped on while the train was still moving slowly. I was surprised that nobody looked worried. The sellers were the most interesting part. A young man walked past me four times with a tray of small chai cups, calling out the same word in a soft, musical voice. An older woman sold bread and boiled eggs from a basket on her head. Near the end of the platform, I noticed an old man sitting alone on a bench. He wasn't waiting for a train. He was just watching, like me. We didn't speak, but he smiled when our eyes met. After about three hours, I walked out into the busy street. My head was full of new sounds and new colours. I had learned something simple but useful: sometimes you understand a city better when you stop and look, instead of trying to go somewhere.

annotations.exampleEn: I tried sushi for the first time last week.
London has many big railway stations.
The platforms were wet after the rain.
The mothers were holding their children's hands very tightly.
The bus was crowded at eight o'clock.
He jumped on the bus just as it was leaving.

annotations.meaningVi: những cốc chai (trà sữa Ấn Độ)
annotations.exampleEn: He was selling chai cups for ten rupees each.
She was calling out the names of fruit.
She carried fruit in a basket on her head.
He wasn't waiting for a train; he was just watching the crowd.
Sometimes you have to stop and look around you.

grammarNotes.title: Past continuous for actions in progress in the past
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì quá khứ tiếp diễn (was/were + V-ing) để mô tả hành động đang diễn ra tại một thời điểm trong quá khứ. Trong bài: 'mothers holding children's hands', 'A young man walked past me… calling out', 'He wasn't waiting for a train. He was just watching.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Past Continuous (was/were + V-ing) for actions happening at a point in the past. From the text: 'He wasn't waiting for a train. He was just watching.'

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect for an earlier learning (one example)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thì quá khứ hoàn thành (had + V3) dùng để chỉ một hành động xảy ra trước một thời điểm khác trong quá khứ. Trong bài: 'I had learned something simple but useful' — việc học diễn ra trước khi anh ấy rời khỏi nhà ga.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Past Perfect (had + past participle) for an action completed before another past moment. From the text: 'I had learned something simple but useful' — the learning happened before he walked out of the station.

translationVi: Khi đến Mumbai lần đầu, tôi đã dành cả một buổi sáng ở một trong những ga tàu lớn của thành phố. Tôi không đi chuyến tàu nào cả. Tôi chỉ muốn quan sát. Sân ga đầy người — học sinh đeo cặp nặng, công nhân áo sơ mi trắng, những bà mẹ nắm tay con rất chặt. Tàu đến vài phút một chuyến, và lúc nào cũng đông. Một số người nhảy lên khi tàu vẫn còn chạy chậm. Tôi ngạc nhiên vì không ai trông có vẻ lo lắng. Người bán hàng rong là phần thú vị nhất. Một thanh niên đi ngang qua tôi bốn lần với khay đầy cốc chai, rao đi rao lại cùng một từ bằng giọng nhẹ, đầy nhạc tính. Một phụ nữ lớn tuổi bán bánh mì và trứng luộc trong cái rổ đội trên đầu. Cuối sân ga, tôi để ý một ông cụ ngồi một mình trên ghế. Ông không đợi tàu. Ông chỉ ngồi quan sát, giống tôi. Chúng tôi không trò chuyện, nhưng ông mỉm cười khi mắt chúng tôi gặp nhau. Sau khoảng ba giờ, tôi bước ra con đường tấp nập. Đầu tôi tràn đầy những âm thanh và màu sắc mới. Tôi đã học được một điều đơn giản nhưng hữu ích: đôi khi ta hiểu một thành phố hơn khi dừng lại và nhìn, thay vì cố đi đâu đó.

questions.prompt: How long did the narrator spend at the station?
options: A whole morning, about three hours
questions.explanation: He spent 'a whole morning' and after 'about three hours' he walked out.
questions.hint: Listen for both 'a whole morning' and 'three hours'.

questions.prompt: Did he take a train that day?
options: Yes, he took one local train
No, he just wanted to watch
questions.explanation: 'I didn't take any train. I just wanted to watch.'

questions.prompt: Which group of people is NOT mentioned on the platforms?
questions.explanation: He mentions students, workers, and mothers — not police officers.
questions.hint: Listen carefully to the list of people on the platform.

questions.prompt: What did some people do as the train was slowing down?
questions.explanation: 'Some people jumped on while the train was still moving slowly.'
questions.hint: Listen for a sudden action with the moving train.

questions.prompt: How many times did the chai seller walk past the narrator?
questions.explanation: 'A young man walked past me four times with a tray of small chai cups.'
questions.hint: Listen for a small number connected to the chai seller.

questions.prompt: How did the chai seller speak?
questions.explanation: 'calling out the same word in a soft, musical voice.'
questions.hint: Listen for two adjectives describing his voice.

questions.prompt: What did the older woman sell?
questions.explanation: 'An older woman sold bread and boiled eggs from a basket on her head.'

questions.prompt: What was the old man on the bench doing?
questions.explanation: 'He wasn't waiting for a train. He was just watching, like me.'
questions.hint: Listen for what he was NOT doing AND what he WAS doing.

questions.prompt: What happened between the narrator and the old man?
options: They smiled at each other when their eyes met
questions.hint: Listen for a small, silent interaction.

questions.prompt: What did the narrator's head feel full of when he left the station?
questions.explanation: 'My head was full of new sounds and new colours.'
questions.hint: Listen for what filled his mind.

questions.prompt: What lesson did the narrator learn?
options: Train travel is the cheapest way to see a city
Sometimes you understand a city better when you stop and look, instead of trying to go somewhere
It is dangerous to talk to strangers in a station
Mumbai trains are always on time
questions.explanation: The final sentence: 'sometimes you understand a city better when you stop and look, instead of trying to go somewhere.'
questions.hint: Listen for the moral at the end.

cloze.template: When I visited Mumbai for the first time, I spent a whole morning at one of the city's big railway stations. I didn't take any train. I just wanted to {{b1}}. The platforms were full of people — students with heavy bags, workers in clean shirts, mothers holding children's hands very {{b2}}. Trains arrived every few minutes, and they were always {{b3}}. Some people jumped on while the train was still moving slowly. I was surprised that nobody looked {{b4}}. The sellers were the most interesting part. A young man walked past me four times with a tray of small chai cups, calling out the same word in a soft, {{b5}} voice. An older woman sold bread and boiled eggs from a {{b6}} on her head. Near the end of the platform, I noticed an old man sitting {{b7}} on a bench. He wasn't waiting for a train. He was just watching, like me. We didn't speak, but he smiled when our eyes {{b8}}. After about three hours, I walked out into the busy street. My head was full of new sounds and new colours. I had learned something simple but useful: sometimes you understand a city better when you {{b9}} and look, instead of trying to go {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: 'watch' — he wanted to observe the station, not travel.
'tightly' — mothers held their children's hands firmly so they wouldn't get lost.
'crowded' — the trains were full of people.
'worried' — the narrator was surprised that nobody looked anxious.
'musical' — describes the soft, song-like voice of the chai seller.
'basket' — a basket carried on her head.
'met' — 'our eyes met' is a fixed expression for two people noticing each other.
'stop' — 'stop and look' is the key idea of the lesson.
'somewhere' — the opposite of standing still is trying to go somewhere.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says he understood the city better by staying still than by moving. Have you ever learned more about a place by simply watching it? Where would you sit in your own city if you wanted to truly see how it works?
